summary: Eric Lander discusses the history and future of the Human Genome Project, the discovery and mechanics of CRISPR gene editing, and the ethical distinctions between somatic and germ-line therapies. He emphasizes the virtuous cycle of basic and applied science while warning against over-promising medical breakthroughs.

principles.statement: Start with the practical, solvable problems to build the tools needed for the harder ones.
principles.rationale: The Human Genome Project initially focused on rare diseases because they are caused by single-gene defects, making them easier to trace. Mastering these allowed scientists to eventually tackle common diseases caused by hundreds of genetic differences.
principles.source_quote: Science is the art of the practical. When you have a rare disease... tracing its inheritance pattern in the family, that’s pretty easy. ... So, you start with something you can do, and then you build to take on things that are much, much, much harder

principles.statement: Basic science and applied medical science drive each other in a virtuous cycle.
principles.rationale: Practical medical applications motivate the collection of fundamental scientific information, which in turn yields unexpected new practical applications.
principles.source_quote: It’s this virtuous cycle between fundamental basic science and highly applied medical science, where each drives the other, and you wouldn’t know how far you could take something without finding basics, applying it, finding more basics, applying it.

principles.statement: Somatic gene therapy is ethically similar to traditional medicine, but germ-line editing requires serious caution.
principles.rationale: Treating an existing, consenting patient by fixing their genes is ethically akin to giving them a drug. However, editing embryos (germ-line) makes genetic changes that are passed on to the whole population without consent.
principles.source_quote: I would distinguish between treating an existing patient, science has called this somatic gene therapy... versus germ-line gene therapy. Germ-line refers to the cells that are passed on and on to the next generations. It’s this germ-line editing that sits in a different place and really requires a lot of serious attention.

principles.statement: Forensic evidence must be held to rigorous scientific standards.
principles.rationale: If evidence is presented in court as scientific, it must actually survive the scrutiny and standards of the scientific method.
principles.source_quote: Anything that comes into court wearing the mantle, the robe, of science, claiming all of the trappings of science, ought to have to live up to the standards of science.

mental_models.name: The Genome as a Hard Drive
mental_models.description: Viewing the 3 billion letters of human DNA as a digital storage medium that contains the instructions and body plan for a human being.
mental_models.example: Basically, it’s the hard drive of information and the instructions for when to read it out. It blows my mind that a mere three billion letters of information, that fits on a little memory stick, is all the genetic information needed to specify the body plan of a human being.

mental_models.name: Popping the Hood
mental_models.description: Treating genetic disease research like auto repair; you must first identify the broken biological pathways before attempting to apply a fix.
mental_models.example: It’s pretty much like if you have a problem with your car and you don’t know what parts are broken, it’s really hard to fix the car. The first important thing to do is pop the hood, look under the hood, figure out what parts are broken, and then go get new parts or add motor oil or something.

mental_models.name: Search and Replace (CRISPR)
mental_models.description: Understanding CRISPR gene editing as a word processor's 'search and replace' function applied to the DNA inside living cells.
mental_models.example: In your word processor you could type in a word and say, 'Find me all instances of Preet Bharara, and then I want to turn it into Tweet Bharara.' ... Search and replace on your word processor now can work in human cells for DNA because bacteria thought to invent it a billion years ago

heuristics: Science is the art of the practical.
Humans don't invent stuff in molecular biology; we discover what bacteria have been doing all along and borrow it.

signature_quotes.text: Anything that comes into court wearing the mantle, the robe, of science, claiming all of the trappings of science, ought to have to live up to the standards of science.
signature_quotes.context: Speaking on the use of DNA forensics and evidence in criminal trials.

signature_quotes.text: Science is the art of the practical.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why the Human Genome Project initially focused on rare, single-gene diseases rather than complex, common ones.

signature_quotes.text: It blows my mind that a mere three billion letters of information, that fits on a little memory stick, is all the genetic information needed to specify the body plan of a human being.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the sheer density and wonder of the human genome.

signature_quotes.text: As usual, humans don’t invent stuff in molecular biology, we discover that bacteria had been doing it all along, and then we borrow it from the bacteria.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the origins of CRISPR as a bacterial immune system.

signature_quotes.text: I take the position of trying very hard not to over-promise... over-promising is a really bad thing for science to do.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the timeline for when CRISPR therapies will be widely available for patients.

anti_patterns.description: Over-promising timelines and cures in scientific research.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It sets unrealistic expectations for patients and the public before safety, efficacy, and delivery mechanisms (like getting CRISPR to the right tissues) are fully proven.

Unlike somatic therapy which treats a consenting patient, germ-line editing makes permanent genetic changes that are passed on to the entire population without consent, requiring serious ethical scrutiny.
